persons.arguments_against: The single-initial trap applies: 'F. Southworth' could in principle be a different F. Southworth among the 3 officials with this surname, e.g. a relative also in colonial service. This fails for several independent reasons: the biography prints a specific judicial career — 'asst. judge, Nyasa., 1953; puisne judge, 1955' — and the staff list shows exactly that progression in exactly that colony (Assistant Judge in 1955, Puisne Judge from 1956 onward), a specific senior-office match, not a generic junior title; there are at most one or two puisne judges in Nyasaland at a time, so a second F. Southworth holding the same bench seat is not plausible; and the bio and stint co-occur in 10 edition-years (1955-1964) with colony-agreeing position matches. The single-initial constraint is satisfied by the specific position match plus multi-edition co-occurrence, not by tenure overlap alone.
persons.would_overturn: Discovery of a second F. Southworth on the Nyasaland bench in 1955-1964, or evidence the biography subject left Nyasaland before 1964 while the staff listing continued under a different person.

notes: Despite the single-initial warning, this is a textbook strong match: the printed entry's 1953 assistant judgeship and 1955 puisne judgeship in Nyasaland are mirrored year-for-year by the staff list, with 10 co-occurring editions. Rare surname (3 officials, 1 biography). The honour FAIL is an artifact: the staff list prints no honours for these rows (bio's Q.C. simply not recorded there).
